posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
I like My clarets well done, and the steaks red.

Yesterday while browsing in the wine store, I was told that a larger part of a private cellar, was just offered for sale, as single bottles, in the “second hand” department.
Bottles from the sixties and backwards. Mostly Bordeaux, and mostly Danish bottled. The owner was an architect, and had a unique temp. controlled, own design, wine storage cellar (not many such, back in the fifties).
Many bottles in 2,3 or 6 of each. Some vintages are weak. Many are 1956, 1957, 1967, 1969.
But also some 1955, 1959, 1962, 1964 and 1949. No first growths, but some 2’-5’ cru, and some known St. Emilion and Pomerols.
All looking very solid, with great fills, good colors, firm capsules, with old tax labels intact.

But when I found this 1929 Ch. Haut-Milon, in a little pile, I bought it immediately.



C-fill, no sepage detected, good cork, tight capsule, bright red color.

Chateau Haut-Milon was a normal, little producer, probably struggling through the late 1920’s. But in 1932, many smaller pauillac producers, created the very first wine-cooperative in Medoc. Sharing machinery and workforces. The name chosen was La Rose Pauillac. They produce several wines today, most known are Haut de La Becade, La Rose Pauillac, and La Fleur Pauillac.

-Chateau Haut-Milon, along with Ch. Haut-St. Lambert were early members, and used the facilities from 1933, to vinify and label their own wines. And I believe it’s still like this today.

I haven’t tasted any Ch. Haut-Milon, young or old yet, but I’m certainly interested now, after I got the old one.

Any information on this chateau, could be nice. And Tn’s too.

Kind regards, Soren.

posts.name: CJ Beazley
posts.cooked: Great post. Were the prices reasonable on the 1960’s stuff? Think you’ll go back and get more?

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
The official list of the wines is not ready yet, but I saw some 1959 La Pointe, Pomerol at $200, 1967 Palmers at $150. -And many others at around $100. Very reasonable prices, and worth trying.
I will go back for more tomorrow, yes.


Also saw a few old Burgs… Mmmm mmm …-old Burgs…aarhhh.
Will post more soon.

Regards, Soren

posts.name: Kristoffer B r y d e r
posts.display_username: Kristoffer B r y d e r

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Visited the shop again as promised.
The most expensive/attractive stuff are
-1959 Carbonnieux (ch. bottled) @ $230,
-1959 La Pointe, Pomerol is only $120,
-1966 Ch. Troplong M. (ch. bottled) @ $130,
-1969 Ducru Beaucaillou @ $120,
-1962 and 64 Clos Fourtet @ $75,
-1962 Calon Segur @ $90,
-1970 Fleur Milon @ $53,
-1949 Ch. Simard @ $75.
And some more. Just from notes taken in the shop.

I got this Cordier Pauillac.




-It is Danish bottled, but the bottle, and capsule are Cordier made. Maybe they had the “wrapping” sent along with the barrels ?
At $53, mature Pauillac, nice fill, color and seals… -Of course I buy.

Hej, Kristoffer.
The shop is Vinoble, Krystalgade (lige ved gl. Daells Varehus), Vinbanken.

-Soren.

posts.name: Kristoffer B r y d e r
posts.cooked: Thank you Søren! I actually thought it could very well be that shop. Last time I was there I picked up two 1985 Côte-Rôtie that have absolutely changed my view of syrah. Will stop by on friday, hoping they have some left!
posts.display_username: Kristoffer B r y d e r

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
You must hurry ! Today I saw the last 1964 Las Cases go, 1967 Palmer -out, and many St. Emilions are gone too.
Btw. The staff tested a lonely 1957 Ducru Beaucaillou (DK bottled), it was very fine, and still alive ! -Good cellar, it seems.

Good luck, and post if You buy some bottles (with pics.)

-Soren.

posts.name: Kristoffer B r y d e r
posts.display_username: Kristoffer B r y d e r

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked:
I have had that 1964 Clos Fourtet some twenty years ago. It was a Justerini & Brooks bottle. The wine was spectacular.

As for 1957, that is my birth year and I own a few bordeaux incl. Haut Brion, Calon Segur and LLC. The vintage was hard as nails some 20 years ago but lately has started to come around. Certainly not a great vintage but the few I’ve recently had were enjoyable.

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
I got the Clos Fourtet’s (62 and 64) on My next-list… I have, and like, 1962 bords, and 1964 are also often surprisingly young. -I will go back, one last time… And only buy these two… And then NO MORE !
-Damn that collectors-gene. - Must- fight- the- urge.

-Soren.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Soren R Nielsen:

I got the Clos Fourtet’s (62 and 64) on My next-list… I have, and like, 1962 bords, and 1964 are also often surprisingly young. -I will go back, one last time… And only buy these two… And then NO MORE !
-Damn that collectors-gene. - Must- fight- the- urge.

-Soren.

That makes sense. Especially the '64. Should be great.

'69 Ducru would be interesting—if it wasn’t '69; one of the few terrible vintages where I’ve never encountered a single wine that could be called an exception to the rule.

'66 Troplong I wouldn’t get too excited about. Having had a bunch of old vintages, inc. '59, '61 and '64 I came to the conclusion that the site always produces rather dull, massive but unexciting wines. They were all sound but not of them remotely interesting.

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Back in the shop today, and was welcomed with an xxx-glass of wine to try (very normal procedure in this store). No infos at all…
-Red core, brown-orange rim, clear, and active legs.
-Initial smell of fresh red apple, just bitten in. -Quickly changing into earth, spices and some sherry nutty aroma. very pleasant smell actually.
-First sip reveals some crisp acidity, but the mild type, the fresh apples again. After some swirling and sipping, the taste gets better, maybe because I adapted to the fresh acid. The hidden spices, and dried fruits started to come out, and just a little hint of sweet too.
An absolutely drinkable bottle of wine, perhaps even good, if served with food. (But not a wine for young girls !)
It was, and I almost guessed it all, from the before mentioned cellar, and weak sixties, -the bad boy, 1969 Ducru Beaucaillou.
A nice treat, from one of the staff (His birth year).

I ended up with only one bottle today.
The recommended 1964 Clos Fourtet, And I chose the best fill (medium A).



-Note the cork length.

On Monday, I will share a 1970 La Fleur Milon, Pauillac (from same case as the one I bought yesterday), with the staff. (Nice guys working there.)

-Soren.

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Glenn, these special cellar sales are not very usual. I wish they were. Many years since I saw one last, and why I write about it.
There are two larger wine stores in Copenhagen, that sells commission wine from private people.
One is called “Skatkammer vin” (treasure chamber wine), The other “Vinbanken” (the wine bank).
They also sell these wines to international customers. I know that Asian collectors often sweep all the Lafites, in one hit, no regards to vintage or fills.
But I guess it’s difficult to ship wine from Denmark to US.

Alfert, I really wanna buy the lot, but that’s $5K+, and I already got too much wine. Stopped racking many years ago, now only wood-box towers. I was bitten twice by the wine bug during the last twenty years, and I don’t want a relapse again. I will probably buy a little more, and hope the rest sells out fast.
I wish You could have some. Like I wish they would bring back the live wine-auctions in Denmark…

Regards, Soren.

posts.name: Kristoffer B r y d e r
posts.cooked:
Soren R Nielsen:

You must hurry ! Today I saw the last 1964 Las Cases go, 1967 Palmer -out, and many St. Emilions are gone too.
Btw. The staff tested a lonely 1957 Ducru Beaucaillou (DK bottled), it was very fine, and still alive ! -Good cellar, it seems.

Good luck, and post if You buy some bottles (with pics.)

-Soren.

Just got back. I guess it is worth mentioning that I have absolutely zero experience with old Bordeaux, I decided to take this opportunity to change that. Funny thing, one of the staff actually mentioned tasting the '69 Ducru you mentioned, the other day.

Since my knowledge of different vintages and chateaux is close to non-existing I chose these bottles based almost solely on fill level, which I think is acceptable for all three.

First, a 1982 Haut-Batailley bought due to my (limited) knowledge of the vintage. Furthermore it is the birth year of my girlfriend, so I can use that as an excuse for buying.

The 1969 La Tour de Pin I bought for its fill level and relatively low price

The 1975 Clos St. Martin I bought also for its fill level and the stained label which meant it was priced below the rest of the batch (means nothing to me).

No signs of seepage or weird colors in any of the wines. I know they are all “lottery tickets” but I just hope to get a picture of what aged lower-level Bordeaux is.

Now for the questions: Can I open these without a Durand? And, more important, how do I prepare them for serving? I would like to open one tomorrow but am unsure if I should have them stand upright for a while due to sediment and if/when I should decant. I hope you guys can help me out
posts.display_username: Kristoffer B r y d e r

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked:
Great pics/picks.
I also got a 1982 Haut-Batailley from that same case, a month ago. Pretty sure it’s solid.

If You take care the 1975 can be opened with careful normal screwing, and the 82’ -no probl.
The DK bottled 1969 St. Em. can be tricky. The worst that can happen is cork on the wine surface, -can be removed easily.

I do not decant, unless its really fine sediments, clouding the wine for hours, after just moving the bottle.
I pour very carefully, and then place the bottle in the latest angled position, on a crumbled towel(from the kitchen). That way I don’t have to get it back upright, and then back down, for later pours.

The French have this low tech wine pourer. Open, and place in holder, one hour before dinner, -gives a little air in the neck, -and sediments settle and stays in the low bottle “corner” (same principle, but easier to handle, and better looking, than My towel.)

So, -stand upright for a day or two, -open maybe 1 hour before (Audouze light), -and pour gently(towel trick).

Many other good advices, can be found.

-Soren.
